ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for egg biryani; A0FJP (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for egg biryani as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of egg biryani as served in a Singapore Indian restaurant or food court, weighing approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704) and hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), composite estimate cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central; National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704) and hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), combined in proportions consistent with a standard egg biryani recipe, and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) reference ranges for rice-based egg dishes. The input record values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against these approved sources. Values are consistent with the approved-source composite range for egg biryani (energy ~140–155 kcal/100g, protein ~4.5–5.5g, fat ~5.5–7g, carbohydrate ~16–19g). Input values retained and attributed to this USDA/ICMR-NIN composite estimate. ICMR-NIN is used as a secondary cross-reference only; primary nutrient evidence is USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 57)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg biryani as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 57 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source: white basmati rice, boiled (GI 57, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The egg and spice components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 10000 = (57 × 17.33 × 450) / 10000 = 44.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 188.9 mg/100 g — below the 200 mg/100 g threshold; saturated fat 2.2 g/100 g is within acceptable limits.
hypertension.guidance: Egg biryani is acceptable for individuals with hypertension at standard portions (≤300 g); avoid adding extra salt at the table and request minimal ghee to prevent saturated fat accumulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 17.3 g/100 g from basmati rice with GI 57 (medium) and dietary fibre only 1.3 g/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 78 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit egg biryani to a half-plate portion (approximately 200–250 g) to keep carbohydrate load under 40 g per meal, pair with a high-fibre raita or vegetable side, and monitor post-prandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 84.4 mg/100 g from hard-boiled eggs; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 380 mg dietary cholesterol, and saturated fat 2.2 g/100 g accumulates with larger portions.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg biryani to a half-plate serving (approximately 200 g) to keep dietary cholesterol below 200 mg per meal, and request preparation with vegetable oil rather than ghee to minimise saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) present in material quantities; saturated fat 2.2 g/100 g is well below the 5 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg biryani is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile; however, those sensitive to nightshades (tomato, green chilli) should monitor individual tolerance and request preparation without these ingredients if needed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Biryani is a fragrant, layered Indian rice dish made with long-grain basmati rice, hard-boiled eggs, and a richly spiced masala base. The preparation follows the traditional dum method: par-cooked basmati rice is layered over a spiced egg masala — typically containing onion, tomato, yogurt, ghee or oil, turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, mint, and green chilli — then sealed and slow-cooked over low heat or steam until the rice absorbs the aromatic flavours and is fully cooked. The result is a moist, fragrant dish with distinct layers of saffron-tinted rice and spiced egg. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 144 kcal, 4.9 g protein, 6.2 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 17.3 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 189 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 57 (medium), driven by basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Clinically, a standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 78 g carbohydrate and approximately 380 mg cholesterol, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The anti-inflammatory spice profile — curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger — offers potential benefit for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Egg Biryani is widely consumed across India, Singapore, and the broader South and Southeast Asian diaspora, and is a staple at Indian restaurants, hawker stalls, and food courts in Singapore. It is halal-certified in most Singapore contexts and is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg biryani?
Is egg biryani high in sodium?
What is the GI of egg biryani?
How much protein in egg biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference entries for cooked basmati rice and hard-boiled egg, and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN ranges for rice-based egg dishes. The original input provenance was grabfood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7); no exact single composite record for egg biryani was available in approved sources, so values were validated by component-level cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for egg biryani as a composite dish is not reported at the composite dish level in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for egg biryani as a composite dish are not reported at the composite level in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. Input ethnic_tags included 'Butter Chicken', 'Noodles', and 'Drinks & Beverages' — these are clearly erroneous tags from the source platform and have been excluded from the output. 4. The input listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; this is retained as the dish contains eggs but no meat (lacto-ovo vegetarian). 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on white basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct composite GI measurement for egg biryani was found in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving (450 g): (57 × 17.33 × 450) / 10000 = 44.5. 7. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 4.89, fat_g 6.22, carbohydrate_g 17.33) are non-zero. 8. Nutrient source: primary evidence is USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source); ICMR-NIN used as cross-reference. The input source listing (grabfood) has been replaced with approved sources per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md source policy. 9. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english: Egg Biryani, Egg Briyani, Egg Dum Biryani, Basmati Egg Biryani, Indian Egg Rice; chinese: 鸡蛋香饭, 雞蛋香飯; malay: Nasi Briyani Telur, Nasi Biryani Telur; tamil: முட்டை பிரியாணி; hindi: अंडा बिरयानी; romanized: Anda Biryani, Muttai Biriyani) are confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array.
